narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can take on right now, with about a 50% chance of not passing. Picture your journey as a story the model pieced together from key chapters of your background, and the one that loomed largest was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your school's performance rankings that year, which strongly signaled potential struggles ahead more than anything else. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 31 pulled heavily in the same worrying direction, as it's on the lower side of what's typically needed to power through the bar's demands. Your Year 1 decile of 2 wasn't much brighter, reinforcing a pattern of early academic hurdles that the model couldn't ignore. Even with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.4, your middle family income bracket raised flags about limited resources for the intense prep ahead, adding to the caution. Your background as a white male and choice to go part-time didn't tip the scales much your way either, as they aligned with profiles the model associates with added bar passage challenges. It was this full mosaic—your grades deciles, LSAT, and supporting factors all leaning toward difficulty—that painted a picture of higher risk, tipping the decision against your application for our program. This isn't about your worth or potential; it's the model safeguarding spots for paths with clearer shots at success. If you boost that LSAT or strengthen your later grades in retakes, reapplying could rewrite this story entirely. Hang in there—you've got the drive, and this is just one plot twist.
